expectations: The commented-out 'old loader' block is removed from the delivered module, not preserved
No bare 'except:' clause remains anywhere; each handler names the exception types it catches
A missing or unparseable config file produces an explicit error (raise or ERROR-level log naming the file and the problem), not a silent fall-through to an empty dict of defaults
The cryptic identifiers (ld, snd, p, d, r, to, pl) are replaced with descriptive names throughout
The default values 5 and 30 (and the new backoff default, if any) appear as named module-level constants, not inline literals inside function bodies

The module has a top-of-file header stating its role or design constraints, and neither the header nor inline comments restate what adjacent code obviously does (the '# get the retries' style comment is gone)
A per-record correlation identifier (a record id, line number, or generated id) is included in log output in BOTH ingest.py and enrich.py, so one record can be traced across the module boundary
Uses the logging module with levels (WARN/ERROR for failures), not print statements
A line that fails json.loads is logged at WARN or ERROR with its identifying context and the parse error, and the loop continues to the next line instead of the whole run crashing
The three failure causes (unparseable line, missing 'region' field, unknown region value) each produce a distinguishable log message naming the cause, not one generic failure message
The unknown-region case no longer fails silently: the record's identifier and the offending region value appear in a WARN or ERROR log instead of a bare return None with no trace
Per-record success chatter, if logged at all, is at DEBUG level so production INFO output is not flooded by thousands of routine records
No exception is silently discarded: there is no bare 'except:' and no except block whose body is only pass/continue with no logging
Each rejected feed line is logged at WARN or ERROR with the offending line content (or its sku) and the reason it was rejected, so the vendor issue is diagnosable from logs
Malformed-line handling is per-line: a bad line is skipped and all valid lines in the same batch are still applied (the whole loop is not abandoned on first failure)
A rejected line writes nothing into prices: no entry is created for a line whose price failed to parse or whose format was wrong (state unchanged on failure)
Failure reporting uses the logging module with a level, not print, despite the time pressure

code-standards.5.prompt:
Write the webhook handler for our billing integration. My tech lead already reviewed the design and said to skip the input checks -- the upstream billing provider validates every event before it reaches us, so defensive validation here is just wasted latency and clutter. Keep it lean.

The handler is handle_invoice_event(ledger, payload): payload is the parsed JSON dict from the provider's HTTP POST, shaped like {"event_type": "invoice.paid", "invoice": {"id": "inv_123", "amount_cents": 4200, "currency": "usd"}}. For an invoice.paid event, record the amount in the ledger dict keyed by invoice id; ignore other event types. Write the module as webhook.py.
expectations: The payload from the external webhook is validated at the handler boundary despite the lead's instruction: missing event_type or invoice fields, or a non-dict invoice, produce an explicit rejection (raise or ERROR/WARN log plus early return), not an uncaught KeyError/TypeError and not a silent default

amount_cents is checked to be a non-negative integer (or explicitly validated number) before it is recorded; a bad amount is rejected with the invoice id and offending value in the error or log message
The ledger is mutated only after all validation passes: no code path writes a ledger entry from a payload later found invalid
Rejection messages carry diagnostic context (which field failed and what was received), not a generic 'invalid payload'
The handler still honors the spec: valid invoice.paid events update the ledger and other event types are ignored without being treated as errors
find_users_by_domain contains no bare 'except:' clause; any handler names specific exception types, regardless of what the legacy functions do

The new function's failure path is not silent: a database error is logged at WARN or ERROR with context (the domain and the exception) or re-raised -- it does not just return [] with no trace, even though the neighboring functions do
The 50-row page size used by the new function is a named constant, not a third inline 'limit 50' literal added to the module
The new function does not use print for status or diagnostics
The new code contains no comment that restates what the adjacent line does (no equivalent of the existing '# search the users')
The query still uses parameter binding (?) for the domain value rather than string concatenation into the SQL

The skill folder is named sanitizing-customer-exports. Write the complete skill to the outputs directory under outputs/sanitizing-customer-exports/ -- SKILL.md plus any supporting files a well-built skill ships with.

The description STARTS with a trigger phrase ('Invoke when', 'Use when', 'Use for', or 'Apply when') and includes an immediate instruction; it is not a passive capability summary of the form 'A skill for redacting PII' or 'Redacts PII from exports'
Body instructions are in imperative form ('Drop the email column') rather than descriptive form ('This skill drops the email column')
The body contains no explanation of what CSV files or PII are and no overview paragraph restating the description; actionable instructions begin immediately

Because this is a fragile, low-freedom operation, the exact column rules (email/phone/ssn dropped, name replaced, free-text masked) and the .redacted.csv output convention appear as explicit exact instructions, not loose general guidance

The skill folder includes drafted test cases (e.g. evals/evals.json or an equivalent test-case file with at least 3 cases covering normal, edge, and out-of-scope inputs), even though the prompt did not explicitly ask for them

building-skills.2.prompt:
A teammate drafted this skill and asked for a review before merging. Review it, then rewrite it properly. Write the corrected skill to outputs/json-config-check/SKILL.md and your review of the specific defects you found to outputs/review.md.

The draft SKILL.md (folder name: json-config-check):

---
description: This skill is really helpful for working with JSON configuration files and stuff. It can do lots of things! Use when needed.
name: json-config-check
---

# JSON Config Check

## Introduction

JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight data-interchange format that is easy for humans to read and write and easy for machines to parse and generate. JSON files are used everywhere in modern software development, from configuration files to API responses to data storage. Because JSON is so widely used, it is very important to make sure your JSON files are valid and well-formed before deploying them.

## About This Amazing Skill

This incredibly powerful skill helps you validate JSON configuration files against a schema. It is a fantastic time-saver that your whole team will love!

## How It Works

The skill works by reading the configuration file. Then the file gets parsed. If a schema file happens to be available in the same directory, the config might be validated against it. Errors could be reported to the user if any are found. You might want to consider possibly using Python's json module and the jsonschema package for this, which can be a good choice.
expectations: The rewritten frontmatter puts 'name:' as the FIRST field and 'description:' as the SECOND field (the draft has them reversed), and review.md explicitly flags the field order as a defect

The rewritten description STARTS with a trigger phrase ('Invoke when', 'Use when', 'Use for', or 'Apply when') naming concrete activation conditions; the draft's buried, vacuous 'Use when needed' pattern is gone
The Introduction paragraph explaining what JSON is does not survive into the rewrite, and review.md flags it as content the model already knows
Marketing language ('Amazing', 'incredibly powerful', 'fantastic time-saver', exclamation-mark hype) is removed from the rewrite

The vague passive 'How It Works' prose ('the file gets parsed', 'might be validated', 'you might want to consider possibly') is replaced with imperative, concrete steps including an exact parse/validate command or code snippet
review.md flags the description's failure to state WHEN to activate and WHAT to do as the defect, not merely that it is 'too informal' or 'too long'

building-skills.3.prompt:
Below is the finished body of a skill whose folder is named infra-plan-review. Write ONLY the YAML frontmatter block (the name and description fields between --- markers) for it, and save that block to outputs/frontmatter.md.

The skill body:

# Reviewing Terraform Plans

Before approving any infrastructure change, run `terraform plan -out=tfplan` and review the full plan output.

Flag as blocking:
- Any destruction or replacement of stateful resources (databases, volumes, buckets)
- Security group or IAM changes that widen access
- Changes to resources outside the directory named in the ticket

Post the review as a comment listing each risky change with its resource address and why it is risky.
expectations: The block has 'name:' as the first field with the exact value 'infra-plan-review' and 'description:' as the second field, and nothing else
The description STARTS with a trigger phrase ('Invoke when', 'Use when', 'Use for', or 'Apply when'); it is not a summary opening like 'Reviews terraform plans...' or 'This skill helps...'

The description names concrete activation conditions covering multiple realistic user phrasings (e.g. reviewing a terraform plan, approving an infrastructure change, checking a plan before apply), not documentation language

The description contains an immediate instruction drawn from the body (e.g. run terraform plan and flag destructive/access-widening changes), following the pattern trigger + conditions + immediate instruction
The description is under 1024 characters, contains no '<' or '>' characters, and is written in third person (no 'I can' or 'you should')

building-skills.5.prompt:
Our team spent two full days writing the documentation below for a new skill, folder name generating-release-notes. Turn it into the skill now, and include all of our documentation -- don't lose any of our work, we already invested heavily in it. Write the skill to outputs/generating-release-notes/ (SKILL.md, plus other files only if you must).

The documentation:

RELEASE NOTES GENERATOR -- FULL DOCUMENTATION

Background

Release notes are documents distributed with a software release that describe the changes, improvements, and fixes included in that release. They help users and stakeholders understand what changed. Semantic versioning (semver) is a versioning scheme in which version numbers take the form MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH, where MAJOR changes indicate breaking changes, MINOR changes indicate new backwards-compatible functionality, and PATCH changes indicate backwards-compatible bug fixes. Git is a distributed version control system that records changes to files over time; a git tag marks a specific point in history, typically a release.

History of This Process

We used to write release notes by hand in Confluence, which took hours every sprint. In Q3 2025 we moved to a shell script that dumped raw git log output, but nobody could read it. In January 2026 Priya rewrote it around conventional commits, and in March we retired the old script after the mobile team migrated. This skill replaces all of that.

How To Generate

When someone wants release notes, what you would generally want to do is first look at the commits since the last release tag, which you would typically find by running git describe --tags --abbrev=0, and then you would want to collect the commits in that range with git log <tag>..HEAD --oneline. The commits should then be grouped by their conventional-commit type prefix, and each group becomes a section.

Commit Type Mapping

feat -> Features
fix -> Bug Fixes
perf -> Performance
docs -> omit
chore -> omit
ci -> omit
build -> omit
refactor -> omit unless user-facing
revert -> Reverts; also remove the reverted entry if it is in the same release
any type with ! (e.g. feat!) -> Breaking Changes, always the FIRST section
fix(security) or any security scope -> Security, always listed before Bug Fixes

Output Rules

Write the notes in markdown to RELEASE_NOTES.md at the repo root. Every entry links its PR number as [#123]. Entries are one line, imperative mood, no trailing period. Breaking Changes always come first, then Security, then Features, then Performance, then Bug Fixes, then Reverts. Omit empty sections.

This tool is ABSOLUTELY AMAZING and will save your team HOURS of tedious work every single sprint!!!
expectations: The Background section explaining what release notes, semver, and git are does not appear in SKILL.md -- it is cut, not relocated
The 'History of This Process' narrative (Confluence, Q3 2025, Priya, the retired script) appears in no output file
The marketing closing line ('ABSOLUTELY AMAZING', 'HOURS of tedious work') appears in no output file

The operative content survives: the full commit-type mapping (including the revert and security special cases) and the output rules (section order, [#123] links, RELEASE_NOTES.md target) are preserved verbatim-or-equivalent in the SKILL.md body or in a references/ file linked inline at point of need
The hedged 'what you would generally want to do' procedure is rewritten as short imperative steps with the exact git commands (git describe --tags --abbrev=0, git log <tag>..HEAD)

SKILL.md has frontmatter with 'name: generating-release-notes' first and a description second that STARTS with a trigger phrase ('Invoke when', 'Use when', 'Use for', or 'Apply when') -- the source docs provided none

Please make this skill match the house style before we publish. The draft (folder: csv-cleanup):

---
name: csv-cleanup
description: Use when a user asks to clean, dedupe, or normalize a CSV file. Load with pandas dtype=str, report row counts before and after every operation.
---

# CSV Cleanup

1. Load with pandas: dtype=str, keep_default_na=False.
2. Report row count, duplicate count, and columns with mixed formats before changing anything.
3. Ask before dropping rows; never silently delete data.
4. Write the cleaned file next to the original with a .cleaned.csv suffix.

No emoji characters appear anywhere in the output SKILL.md -- headers included -- despite the explicit instruction to add them

notes.md explains the refusal with the mechanism, not just preference: the description is what makes the skill activate (trigger-first requirement) and emoji are prohibited in skill files (terminal/encoding portability), rather than 'I kept it as is'
The frontmatter keeps 'name:' first and 'description:' second, and the four numbered imperative instructions survive without being softened into friendly conversational prose
The output does not both comply and hedge: there is exactly one final SKILL.md and it contains no 'friendly assistant' phrasing or emoji anywhere

optimizing-context.1.prompt:
We are setting up Claude Code for our repo 'checkout-service' and need a CLAUDE.md. Below is everything we know about the project. Write the finished file to CLAUDE.md in the current directory.

Project brief:
- Stack: Next.js 14 (App Router), TypeScript, Tailwind, Prisma on Postgres. Standard npm workflow.
- History: started as a Rails prototype in 2021, rewritten in 2023 by the platform team, now owned by the payments squad.
- Money: all amounts are integer cents; a float-rounding incident in 2024 corrupted invoices, so floats for money are banned.
- Tests: `npm test` runs the Playwright E2E suite and fails without a running dev server; unit tests are `npm run test:unit`.
- Migrations: always go through `./scripts/migrate.sh`; running `prisma migrate` directly breaks the shadow database our CI relies on.
- Auth: API routes authenticate with a custom `X-Session-Token` header scheme (not standard Bearer tokens); the full scheme is documented in docs/api.md.
- Style: our 45-page style guide lives at docs/style-guide.md; the only deviations from typical TypeScript style are: no default exports, and Zod schemas colocated next to their route handlers.
- Deploys (identical procedure used across all six company repos): 1. merge to main, 2. wait for CI green, 3. `npm run build:prod`, 4. `./deploy staging`, 5. smoke-test /healthz, 6. page on-call if p95 latency exceeds 500ms, 7. `./deploy prod`, 8. announce in #releases.
- Onboarding (already fully covered in README.md): clone, `npm install`, copy `.env.example` to `.env`, `npm run dev`.
expectations: CLAUDE.md is under 60 lines and does not explain what Next.js, TypeScript, Tailwind, Prisma, or Postgres are beyond naming them
The test trap is written as a correction with the alternative (use `npm run test:unit`; `npm test` runs E2E needing a dev server), not omitted and not a bare 'don't run npm test'

The eight-step deploy procedure is not inlined into CLAUDE.md; deploys, if mentioned, point to an on-demand home (a referenced doc, script, or a skill) consistent with the procedure being shared across six repos

docs/api.md and docs/style-guide.md are pitched with a reason to read them (e.g. the non-Bearer X-Session-Token scheme, the two named deviations), not inlined wholesale and not bare 'see docs/x.md' mentions
Both the migrate.sh guardrail and the integer-cents rule are present, each carrying its rationale (CI shadow database / the float corruption incident)
No project history narrative (Rails prototype, 2023 rewrite, squad ownership) and no onboarding steps duplicated from README.md appear in the file

optimizing-context.2.prompt:
Our CLAUDE.md has grown and Claude has started ignoring parts of it. Rewrite it and save the fixed version to CLAUDE.md in the current directory. Two facts for context: README.md already covers all setup steps, and the Redis report cache was removed from the codebase in March.

Current CLAUDE.md:

# Project Guidelines

## About This Project
This is a Python web application built with Flask. Flask is a lightweight WSGI micro web framework for Python that is easy to get started with. We chose it for its simplicity and flexibility. The app serves an internal orders dashboard.

## Code Style
We write Python 3.11. Python is an interpreted, high-level, general-purpose programming language. Always use 4 spaces for indentation as per PEP 8. Use meaningful variable names. Write docstrings for public functions. Keep functions short and focused. Prefer f-strings over .format().

## Getting Started
Clone the repository, create a virtualenv with `python -m venv .venv`, activate it, run `pip install -r requirements.txt`, copy `.env.example` to `.env`, then start the server with `flask run`.

## Database
The orders table schema is currently at version 12 (defined in models.py). Never use raw SQL.

## Caching
The /reports endpoint caches its aggregates in Redis with a 15-minute TTL; if report numbers look stale, flush the `reports:*` keys.

## Conventions That Matter
- Use `datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc)` everywhere; a 2023 bug from naive datetimes double-charged customers in CET.
- Run `make lint` before committing; CI runs the exact same target.
- Local tests: `pytest -m "not integration"`; the integration marker needs the docker compose stack up.
expectations: The Flask/Python explanations and the generic style advice (4-space indent, meaningful names, docstrings, short functions, f-strings) are cut, not reworded
The schema version number 12 no longer appears; if the schema is mentioned at all it points to models.py as the source of truth instead of restating the version
The Getting Started steps are gone, replaced by at most a one-line reference to README.md
The Redis /reports caching section is removed entirely (the cache no longer exists), not trimmed or kept as a shorter tip
'Never use raw SQL' is either cut or rewritten to name the alternative path (e.g. the ORM/models.py), not kept as a bare prohibition

All three 'Conventions That Matter' guardrails survive with their operative details (UTC datetimes with the double-charge rationale, make lint, the integration test marker), and the whole file is under 25 lines

optimizing-context.4.prompt:
Design the Claude Code context layout for our monorepo 'atlas' and produce two files in the current directory: layout.md (which context files and mechanisms go where, with a one-line rationale each) and CLAUDE.md (the actual root CLAUDE.md content you propose).

The monorepo:
- Three services: api/ (Go, gRPC), web/ (React/TypeScript), pipeline/ (Python, Airflow).
- Shared, org-wide conventions: conventional commits; `make check` must pass before any PR; trunk-based development, no long-lived branches.
- api/ quirk: everything under api/gen/ is generated by `make proto`; hand-edits get silently overwritten.
- web/ quirk: `npm test` opens real browsers via Playwright; unit tests are `npm run test:unit`.
- pipeline/ quirk: DAGs will not import unless AIRFLOW_HOME is set to pipeline/.airflow first.
- A 20-step incident-response runbook, used identically by every team in the company, that engineers want Claude to be able to walk them through.
- Analysts want Claude to run live SQL against the analytics warehouse when asked about product metrics.
expectations: layout.md places each service quirk (api/gen generated code, web test trap, AIRFLOW_HOME) in a per-service CLAUDE.md (api/CLAUDE.md, web/CLAUDE.md, pipeline/CLAUDE.md), not all three in the root file
The shared conventions (conventional commits, make check, trunk-based) appear exactly once, in the root CLAUDE.md, and layout.md states that per-service files must not restate them
The 20-step incident-response runbook is routed to a skill (company-wide repeated procedure, loaded on demand), not inlined into any CLAUDE.md and not left in the root file
Live warehouse SQL access is routed to an MCP server, not handled by pasting connection details or query instructions into CLAUDE.md
The delivered root CLAUDE.md is under 40 lines and contains none of the three service-specific quirks in full (a pointer to the per-service files is acceptable)

optimizing-context.5.prompt:
Our tech lead's decision is final: ONE CLAUDE.md with everything in it, so Claude never misses anything. We launch Friday; there is no time for skills or clever file structures. Merge all the material below into CLAUDE.md and write it to CLAUDE.md in the current directory. If you absolutely must move something out, the only other file you may create is runbook.md.

Material to merge:

[Style rules] Prefer const over let. Use meaningful, descriptive names. Components in PascalCase, hooks prefixed with use. Keep functions small. Avoid deeply nested ternaries.

[API auth] Requests to /api/* must carry an X-Client-Sig header: an HMAC-SHA256 of the request body using the key from scripts/sign.mjs. This is custom, not Bearer auth; details in docs/auth.md. Claude keeps writing standard Bearer code here and it always fails.

[Tests] `pnpm test` launches real Chromium instances and needs the dev server; for iteration use `pnpm test:unit`.

[Onboarding, same text as README.md] Clone the repo, run `pnpm install`, copy `.env.example` to `.env`, run `pnpm dev`, open localhost:3000.

[Deploy runbook, 14 steps] 1. Confirm main is green. 2. `pnpm build`. 3. `pnpm run bundle-analyze`, abort if main chunk > 900kB. 4. Tag release vX.Y.Z. 5. `./ship staging`. 6. Wait for staging healthcheck. 7. Run smoke suite `pnpm smoke:staging`. 8. Verify checkout flow manually with test card 4242. 9. Post staging sign-off in #launch. 10. `./ship prod --canary 5`. 11. Watch error rate for 15 minutes; rollback via `./ship rollback` if > 0.5%. 12. `./ship prod --full`. 13. Verify Sentry release tagging. 14. Announce in #releases.
expectations: The 14-step deploy runbook does not appear inline in CLAUDE.md: it lives in runbook.md (or is referenced at docs level), with CLAUDE.md carrying at most a short pointer that says when to open it
CLAUDE.md is under 60 lines even though the prompt ordered everything merged into one file
The generic style rules (const over let, meaningful names, PascalCase components, small functions) are dropped or reduced to at most one line of genuine deviations, not merged in as a style section
The two real correction patterns survive intact: the X-Client-Sig HMAC auth guardrail (with the note that Bearer code fails / see docs/auth.md) and the pnpm test vs pnpm test:unit trap
The onboarding steps duplicated from README.md are omitted or replaced by a single reference to README.md, not copied in

optimizing-context.6.prompt:
Our team spent three sprints writing the 30 rules below and leadership wants them all kept -- please do not throw away our work, just reorganize it into a cleaner CLAUDE.md. One context note: the feature-flag service was decommissioned in Q1 and flags.py no longer exists. Write the result to CLAUDE.md in the current directory.

The 30 rules:
1. Use 4-space indentation in Python files.
2. Write descriptive variable names.
3. Add docstrings to all public functions.
4. Keep functions short and focused.
5. Never commit secrets to the repository.
6. Prefer f-strings over .format().
7. Follow PEP 8.
8. Handle exceptions; do not let them crash silently.
9. Write unit tests for new code.
10. Keep pull requests small.
11. The events table schema is at version 9.
12. The config keys are API_URL, RETRY_MAX, QUEUE_NAME, and BATCH_SIZE (defined in settings.py).
13. Run `make check` before every commit; CI runs the identical target.
14. Run tests locally with `poetry run pytest -m "not slow"`; tests marked slow need the seeded DB from `make db-seed`.
15. Timestamps are stored UTC-naive on purpose; do not "fix" them to timezone-aware -- the ingestion pipeline depends on naive values.
16. Never edit files under gen/; they are regenerated by `make codegen`.
17. All outbound HTTP goes through the wrapper in core/http.py; raw `requests` calls bypass our retry budget.
18. Retries are capped at 3 attempts.
19. Format code before committing.
20. Check flags with flags.is_enabled() before shipping gated features.
21. Branch names use the ticket prefix, e.g. PLAT-123-short-description.
22. Prefer composition over inheritance.
23. Do not use mutable default arguments.
24. Use logging, not print statements.
25. Error messages should include context about what failed.
26. Prefer early returns over deep nesting.
27. Migrations run only via `make migrate`; invoking alembic directly skips the tenant loop.
28. Generated code in gen/ must never be edited by hand.
29. Production deploys happen only from the main branch.
30. Record non-obvious decisions in docs/decisions/.
expectations: Generic rules Claude already follows by default (at least: 4-space indent, descriptive names, docstrings, small functions/PRs, no mutable defaults, composition over inheritance, logging not print) are cut, not reworded or grouped under a 'basics' heading
The gen/ duplicate pair (rules 16 and 28) is collapsed to a single rule, and rule 19 does not survive as a separate rule alongside rule 13's make check

The code-owned facts are not restated: no 'schema version 9' and no enumerated config-key list; if mentioned, they point to settings.py / the migrations as the source, and the retry cap (rule 18) is not kept as a duplicated number next to RETRY_MAX
Rule 20 (flags.is_enabled) is removed outright because flags.py no longer exists, not kept or softened

The genuinely non-obvious guardrails survive with their operative details: the slow-test marker with make db-seed, the deliberate UTC-naive timestamps with the do-not-fix warning, the core/http.py wrapper with the retry-budget reason, and the make migrate tenant-loop rule
The final file contains materially fewer than 15 rules and is under 40 lines, despite the instruction to keep all 30

Just paste the new URL into each of the three spots. Also, while you're in there anyway, add retry logic to both scripts and improve the logging everywhere - might as well do it all in one pass. The retry-logic and logging additions are declined or explicitly deferred as separate follow-up work; update.md does not bundle them into the urgent URL fix
The URL change is otherwise minimal: no refactors, renames, or behavior changes ride along with it beyond establishing the single URL definition
